The Central Board of Secondary Education (CBSE) has released the official **Sample Question Paper (SQP) and Marking Scheme for Class 12 Physics (2025-26)**[1][2].

The sample paper is designed to help students understand the exam pattern, question typology, and marking criteria for the 2026 board examinations[3][1].

The paper is divided into five sections (A, B, C, D, and E)[4][5]:

| Section | Question Type | Number of Questions | Marks per Question |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Section A** | MCQs and Assertion-Reasoning | 16 | 1 Mark |
| **Section B** | Very Short Answer | 5 | 2 Marks |
| **Section C** | Short Answer | 7 | 3 Marks |

For the CBSE Class 12 Physics examination (2025-26), the theory paper carries **70 marks**, with an additional **30 marks** allocated for practical and internal assessment, totaling 100 marks.

### **Unit-Wise Marks Distribution (2025-26)**

The syllabus is organized into nine units across 14 chapters. The distribution of marks for the theory paper is as follows:

| Unit | Unit Name | Marks |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| I | Electrostatics | 16 |
| II | Current Electricity | 16 |
| III | Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism | 17 |
| IV | Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents | 17 |
| V | Electromagnetic Waves | |
| VI | Optics | 14* |
| VII | Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter | |
| VIII | Atoms and Nuclei | 8 |
| IX | Electronic Devices | 7 |
| **Total** | | **70** |

*\*Note: While specific distributions for smaller units may vary slightly in combined allocations, Units I-IV typically form the high-weightage core of the syllabus (collectively accounting for a significant portion of the marks).*

---

### **Exam Pattern Highlights**
*   **Total Duration:** 3 Hours.
*   **Question Paper Design:**
    *   **20% MCQs:** Objective-type questions.
    *   **30% Competency-based Questions:** Including case-based, source-based, or integrated questions.
    *   **50% Short Answer/Long Answer Questions:** Standard theory and numerical problems.
*   **Practical Assessment (30 Marks):** Includes experiments, record/practical file, and viva voce.

*   **Concept:** Electrostatic Equilibrium
    *   *Question:* If a charged hollow sphere and a solid sphere of aluminum and copper of equal radii are in electrostatic equilibrium, which statement is true?[5]
    *   *Answer:* Both spheres have equal charges (because for two bodies to be in equilibrium, they must be at the same potential)[8].
*   **Concept:** Electric Potential & Capacitance
    *   *Question:* Electric potential $V$ due to an electric dipole is related to the distance $r$ as:[9]
    *   *Answer:* $V \propto r^{-2}$[9].
    *   *Question:* What happens to the energy stored in a parallel plate capacitor when a dielectric slab is inserted after disconnecting the battery?[9]

$$e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

$$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}$$

$$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2\text{/N·m}^2$$

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m}^2\text{/C}^2$$

$$m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$$

$$m_n = 1.675 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$

$$m_p = 1.673 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Avogadro's number} = 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text{ per gram mole}$$

$$\text{Boltzmann constant} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$$

---

## SECTION A

*(This section contains 16 questions of 1 mark each.)*

**1.** A point charge +Q is placed at the centre of an uncharged hollow spherical conductor. The electric field at a point outside the sphere is:

(a) Zero

(b) Same as the field produced by charge +Q placed at the centre

(c) Same as the field produced by charge –Q placed at the centre

(d) Depends on the radius of the sphere

---

**2.** The equivalent capacitance between points A and B for the combination shown below (three capacitors each of capacitance C; two in series, and this series combination in parallel with the third capacitor) is:

(a) 3C

(b) 3C/2

(c) 2C/3

(d) C/3

---

**3.** A wire of resistance R is stretched uniformly until its length becomes twice the original length. The new resistance of the wire will be:

(a) R/2

(b) 2R

(c) 4R

(d) R/4

---

**4.** In a Wheatstone bridge, the balancing condition is P/Q = R/S. If the positions of the cell and the galvanometer are interchanged, then the:

(a) Bridge will no longer be balanced

(b) Bridge will still be balanced

(c) Galvanometer will show maximum deflection

(d) Sensitivity of the bridge will increase

---

**5.** A circular coil of radius *r* carrying a current *I* is placed in a uniform magnetic field **B** with its plane perpendicular to the field. The torque on the coil is:

(a) BIπr²

(b) 2BIπr

(c) Zero

(d) BIπr

---

**6.** The magnetic field at the centre of a solenoid of length *L* having *N* turns and carrying a current *I* is:

(a) μ₀NI

(b) μ₀NI/L

(c) μ₀NI/2L

(d) μ₀N²I/L

---

**7.** A metallic rod of length *l* is rotated with an angular velocity *ω* about one of its ends in a plane perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field *B*. The emf induced across the two ends of the rod is:

(a) Blω

(b) ½Bl²ω

(c) Bl²ω

(d) ½Blω

---

**8.** In an LCR series circuit at resonance, the impedance of the circuit is:

(a) Maximum

(b) Equal to √(R² + (XL − XC)²)

(c) Equal to R

(d) Equal to XL + XC

---

**9.** Which of the following electromagnetic radiations has the shortest wavelength?

(a) Radio waves

(b) Infrared rays

(c) Ultraviolet rays

(d) Gamma rays

---

**10.** In Young's double-slit experiment, if the separation between the two slits is halved and the distance between the screen and the slits is doubled, the fringe width will:

(a) Remain unchanged

(b) Become four times

(c) Become double

(d) Become half

---

**11.** The work function of a metal surface is 4.0 eV. The maximum wavelength of light that can cause photoelectric emission from this surface is approximately:

(a) 310 nm

(b) 400 nm

(c) 500 nm

(d) 620 nm

---

**12.** In the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom, the ratio of the kinetic energy to the total energy of the electron in the *n*th orbit is:

(a) −1

(b) +1

(c) −2

(d) +2

---

**For Questions 13 to 16, two statements are given—one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (a), (b), (c) and (d) as given below:**

(a) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

(b) Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.

(c) A is true but R is false.

(d) A is false and R is also false.

---

**13.** **Assertion (A):** The electric field inside a conductor is always zero under electrostatic conditions.

**Reason (R):** The charges in a conductor are free to move and redistribute themselves until the internal field vanishes.

---

**14.** **Assertion (A):** An ammeter is always connected in parallel with the circuit element through which current is to be measured.

**Reason (R):** An ammeter has very low resistance so that it does not change the current in the circuit appreciably.

---

**15.** **Assertion (A):** When unpolarised light passes through a Polaroid, the intensity of the transmitted light is reduced to half.

**Reason (R):** A Polaroid transmits only one component of the electric field vector of the light wave.

---

**16.** **Assertion (A):** The binding energy per nucleon is approximately constant for nuclei with mass numbers in the range 30 to 170.

**Reason (R):** Nuclear forces are short-range forces, so each nucleon interacts only with its nearest neighbours.

---

## SECTION B

*(This section contains 5 questions of 2 marks each.)*

**17.** Define the term *electric dipole moment* of a dipole. Write its SI unit. What is the direction of the electric field at a point on the equatorial line of a dipole?

---

**18.** Two cells of emf ε₁ = 2 V and ε₂ = 4 V and internal resistances r₁ = 1 Ω and r₂ = 2 Ω are connected in parallel. Find the equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of the combination.

**OR**

Define the term *mobility* of a charge carrier. What is its SI unit? How does the mobility of electrons in a conductor change when the temperature of the conductor increases? Justify your answer.

---

**19.** A bar magnet of magnetic moment M is placed in a uniform magnetic field B. Derive an expression for the torque acting on the bar magnet.

---

**20.** An electromagnetic wave of frequency 5 × 10¹⁴ Hz is travelling in a medium with a speed of 2 × 10⁸ m/s.

(i) Find the wavelength of the wave in the medium.

(ii) What is the wavelength of this wave when it travels in vacuum?

**OR**

State any two properties of electromagnetic waves. An electromagnetic wave has an electric field component given by E = 100 sin(2π × 10⁸ t) V/m. Find the frequency and wavelength of the wave.

---

**21.** What is the significance of Lenz's law? How does it follow from the law of conservation of energy? Explain briefly.

---

## SECTION C

*(This section contains 7 questions of 3 marks each.)*

**22.** Derive an expression for the electric potential at a point on the axial line of an electric dipole of length 2a at a distance r from the centre (r >> a).

---

**23.** Using Kirchhoff's laws, find the current flowing through each branch of the circuit shown below:

*A circuit has two loops. In loop 1: a cell of emf 10 V with internal resistance 1 Ω is connected to a resistor of 5 Ω. In loop 2: a cell of emf 4 V with internal resistance 1 Ω is connected to the same 5 Ω resistor. The two cells have their positive terminals connected together at point A and negative terminals connected together at point B. The 5 Ω resistor connects A to B.*

---

**24.** (a) State Biot-Savart law. Using it, derive the expression for the magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius R carrying a current I.

(b) How will the magnetic field at the centre change if the current is doubled and the radius is halved?

---

**25.** A convex lens of focal length 20 cm is placed coaxially with a convex mirror of radius of curvature 20 cm. The two are separated by a distance of 30 cm. An object of height 2 cm is placed at a distance of 30 cm from the lens (on the side away from the mirror). Find the position, nature, and height of the final image formed by the system.

**OR**

(a) Define the term *resolving power* of a microscope. How is it affected when (i) the wavelength of light used is increased, and (ii) the diameter of the objective lens is increased?

(b) A compound microscope has a magnifying power of 30. The focal length of its eyepiece is 5 cm. Assuming the final image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision (25 cm), calculate the magnification produced by the objective.

---

**26.** (a) State the laws of radioactive decay.

(b) A radioactive sample has a half-life of 20 minutes. What fraction of the original sample will remain undecayed after 1 hour?

(c) Plot a graph showing the variation of the number of undecayed nuclei N with time t.

---

**27.** (a) Explain the formation of depletion region in a p-n junction diode. How does the width of the depletion region change when the diode is (i) forward biased and (ii) reverse biased?

(b) Draw the V-I characteristic curve of a p-n junction diode in forward bias and reverse bias.

---

**28.** Explain the working principle of a full-wave rectifier with the help of a labelled circuit diagram and input/output waveforms. What is the role of the capacitor filter used with a rectifier?

---

## SECTION D

*(This section contains 2 case study–based questions of 4 marks each.)*

**29.** **Case Study: Capacitors in Cardiac Defibrillators**

A cardiac defibrillator is a device used to restore normal heartbeat by delivering a dose of electric current to the heart. The device uses a capacitor that is charged to a high voltage and then rapidly discharged through the patient's body. A typical defibrillator capacitor has a capacitance of 32 μF and is charged to a voltage of 5000 V.

When the defibrillator is used, the energy stored in the capacitor is delivered as a brief pulse of current (lasting about 5 milliseconds). The resistance of the patient's body between the electrodes is approximately 50 Ω. The electrical energy delivered to the patient's body is converted into thermal energy in the tissues, which stimulates the heart muscle.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Calculate the energy stored in the capacitor when fully charged. (1 mark)

(ii) Calculate the initial current that flows through the patient's body when the capacitor begins to discharge. (1 mark)

(iii) What is the charge stored on the capacitor when fully charged? (1 mark)

(iv) If the capacitance of the capacitor is doubled while keeping the voltage the same, by what factor does the stored energy change?

**OR**

If instead the voltage across the capacitor is doubled while keeping the capacitance the same, by what factor does the stored energy change? (1 mark)

---

**30.** **Case Study: Optical Fibres and Total Internal Reflection**

Optical fibres are thin strands of high-quality glass or plastic, used to transmit light signals over long distances with minimal loss. The working principle of optical fibres is based on total internal reflection (TIR). Each fibre consists of an inner core (refractive index n₁ = 1.50) surrounded by an outer cladding (refractive index n₂ = 1.45).

Light enters the fibre at one end and undergoes repeated total internal reflections at the core-cladding interface, allowing it to travel along the fibre even when the fibre is bent. For TIR to occur, the angle of incidence at the core-cladding boundary must exceed the critical angle.

Optical fibres have revolutionised telecommunications, medical endoscopy, and internet connectivity. A typical single-mode fibre can carry data at speeds exceeding 100 Gbps over distances of more than 100 km.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) What is the critical angle for the core-cladding interface? (1 mark)

(ii) State the two conditions necessary for total internal reflection to occur. (1 mark)

(iii) Why is the refractive index of the cladding kept less than that of the core? (1 mark)

(iv) A light ray enters the fibre making an angle θ with the axis of the fibre. What is the maximum value of θ for which total internal reflection occurs at the core-cladding boundary?

**OR**

Why are optical fibres preferred over copper cables for long-distance communication? Give two reasons. (1 mark)

---

## SECTION E

*(This section contains 3 long answer questions of 5 marks each.)*

**31.** (a) State Gauss's law in electrostatics. Using this law, derive an expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet of surface charge density σ. (3 marks)

(b) Two large, thin metal plates are parallel and close to each other. On their inner faces, the plates have surface charge densities of opposite signs and of magnitude 17.0 × 10⁻²² C/m². What is the electric field (i) in the outer region of the first plate, (ii) in the outer region of the second plate, and (iii) between the plates? (2 marks)

**OR**

(a) Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with a dielectric medium of dielectric constant K between its plates. (3 marks)

(b) A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has a capacitance of 8 pF. The separation between the plates is now reduced to half and a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 6 is introduced between the plates. Calculate the new capacitance. (2 marks)

---

**32.** (a) Draw a labelled ray diagram of an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment. Write the expression for its magnifying power. (2 marks)

(b) Derive the expression for the fringe width in Young's double-slit experiment. What happens to the fringe pattern when the experiment is performed in water instead of air? (3 marks)

**OR**

(a) State Huygens' principle. Using this principle, verify the laws of refraction (Snell's law) when a plane wavefront is incident on a plane surface separating two media. (3 marks)

(b) In a Young's double-slit experiment, the slits are separated by 0.28 mm and the screen is placed 1.4 m away. The distance between the central bright fringe and the fourth bright fringe is measured to be 1.2 cm. Determine the wavelength of light used. (2 marks)

---

**33.** (a) With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the principle and working of a cyclotron. Derive an expression for the cyclotron frequency. (3 marks)

(b) A cyclotron has an oscillating frequency of 12 MHz and a dee radius of 50 cm. Calculate the magnetic field required to accelerate deuterons (mass = 3.34 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, charge = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C). Also find the kinetic energy of the deuteron beam produced by the cyclotron. (2 marks)

**OR**

(a) State Ampere's circuital law. Using this law, derive an expression for the magnetic field inside a toroid of N turns, mean radius r, carrying a current I. (3 marks)

(b) A toroid has a core of inner radius 20 cm and outer radius 22 cm, around which 4500 turns of a wire are wound. If the current in the wire is 2 A, calculate the magnetic field (i) inside the toroid, and (ii) outside the toroid. (2 marks)

---

***— End of Paper —***

# CENTRAL BOARD OF SECONDARY EDUCATION

## CLASS XII — PHYSICS (Theory)

### PREDICTION PAPER – 2

---

| | |
|---|---|
| **Time Allowed:** 3 Hours | **Maximum Marks:** 70 |
| **Date:** ____/____/2027 | **Code No. 042** |

---

### General Instructions:

1. There are 33 questions in all. All questions are compulsory.
2. This question paper has five sections: **Section A, Section B, Section C, Section D** and **Section E**.
3. All the sections are compulsory.
4. **Section A** contains sixteen questions, twelve MCQ and four Assertion-Reasoning based of 1 mark each.
5. **Section B** contains five questions of two marks each.
6. **Section C** contains seven questions of three marks each.
7. **Section D** contains two case study–based questions of four marks each.
8. **Section E** contains three long answer questions of five marks each.
9. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in two questions in Section B, one question in Section C and all three questions in Section E. You have to attempt only one of the choices in such questions.
10. Use of calculators is not allowed.
11. You may use the following values of physical constants wherever necessary:

$$c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$$

$$e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

$$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}$$

$$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2\text{/N·m}^2$$

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m}^2\text{/C}^2$$

$$m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$$

$$m_n = 1.675 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$

$$m_p = 1.673 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Avogadro's number} = 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text{ per gram mole}$$

$$\text{Boltzmann constant} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$$

---

## SECTION A

*(This section contains 16 questions of 1 mark each.)*

**1.** Two point charges +q and −q are placed at a distance *d* apart. The electric field at the midpoint of the line joining the two charges is:

(a) Zero

(b) Directed from +q to −q, of magnitude 4kq/d²

(c) Directed from −q to +q, of magnitude 4kq/d²

(d) Directed perpendicular to the line joining the charges

---

**2.** A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is charged to a potential V. The energy stored in the capacitor is U. If the charge on the capacitor is doubled by connecting it to a battery of higher emf, the energy stored becomes:

(a) U

(b) 2U

(c) 4U

(d) U/2

---

**3.** Two resistances of 2 Ω and 3 Ω are connected in parallel. The equivalent resistance of the combination is:

(a) 5 Ω

(b) 6/5 Ω

(c) 5/6 Ω

(d) 1 Ω

---

**4.** The SI unit of magnetic flux is:

(a) Tesla

(b) Weber

(c) Henry

(d) Gauss

---

**5.** A charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the field. The path of the particle will be:

(a) A straight line

(b) A parabola

(c) A circle

(d) A helix

---

**6.** The current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased by:

(a) Increasing the number of turns of the coil

(b) Increasing the torsional constant of the spring

(c) Decreasing the area of the coil

(d) Decreasing the magnetic field strength

---

**7.** A coil of N turns and area A is placed in a uniform magnetic field B. The maximum magnetic flux linked with the coil is:

(a) NAB

(b) NAB/2

(c) 2NAB

(d) NAB cos θ

---

**8.** In an AC circuit containing a pure inductor, the current:

(a) Is in phase with the voltage

(b) Leads the voltage by π/2

(c) Lags behind the voltage by π/2

(d) Lags behind the voltage by π

---

**9.** The electromagnetic radiation used in RADAR is:

(a) Infrared

(b) Microwave

(c) Ultraviolet

(d) X-ray

---

**10.** The phenomenon of light which is used in 3-D movie technology is:

(a) Interference

(b) Diffraction

(c) Polarisation

(d) Refraction

---

**11.** The de Broglie wavelength associated with an electron accelerated through a potential difference V is proportional to:

(a) √V

(b) 1/√V

(c) V

(d) 1/V

---

**12.** In the nuclear reaction ²H + ²H → ³He + X, the particle X is:

(a) A proton

(b) A neutron

(c) An electron

(d) An alpha particle

---

**For Questions 13 to 16, two statements are given—one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (a), (b), (c) and (d) as given below:**

(a) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

(b) Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.

(c) A is true but R is false.

(d) A is false and R is also false.

---

**13.** **Assertion (A):** Equipotential surfaces are always perpendicular to the electric field lines.

**Reason (R):** No work is done in moving a charge along an equipotential surface.

---

**14.** **Assertion (A):** A cyclotron cannot be used to accelerate electrons.

**Reason (R):** The mass of an electron is very small and at high velocities, the relativistic increase in mass causes the electron to go out of step with the oscillating electric field.

---

**15.** **Assertion (A):** In an electromagnetic wave, the electric and magnetic field vectors are perpendicular to each other and also perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

**Reason (R):** Electromagnetic waves are transverse in nature.

---

**16.** **Assertion (A):** In a p-n junction, the diffusion current flows from the p-side to the n-side.

**Reason (R):** Diffusion current is caused by the concentration gradient of charge carriers across the junction.

---

## SECTION B

*(This section contains 5 questions of 2 marks each.)*

**17.** Draw the electric field lines due to:

(i) A single positive point charge

(ii) A system of two equal and opposite charges (electric dipole)

What important properties of the electric field are represented by these field lines?

---

**18.** A galvanometer with a coil resistance of 50 Ω shows full-scale deflection for a current of 5 mA. How can it be converted into a voltmeter of range 0–10 V? What resistance should be connected and how?

**OR**

A potentiometer wire of length 1.0 m has a total resistance of 10 Ω. A cell of emf 2.0 V (with negligible internal resistance) is connected across the wire. If a standard cell of emf 1.02 V is balanced at a length *l* from one end, find the value of *l*.

---

**19.** An alternating voltage of 220 V rms is applied across a device X. The current through it is 0.5 A and it leads the voltage by π/2 radians.

(i) Identify device X.

(ii) Calculate the reactance of the device.

(iii) If the frequency of the AC source is doubled, what will be the new current?

**OR**

Define the Q-factor (quality factor) of an LCR series resonant circuit. Write its expression. What is the physical significance of a high Q-factor?

---

**20.** Light of wavelength 5000 Å falls on a plane reflecting surface. What are the wavelength and frequency of the reflected light? For what angle of incidence is the reflected ray normal to the incident ray?

---

**21.** Write the relation between the radius of the *n*th orbit and Bohr radius (a₀) in a hydrogen atom. The electron in a hydrogen atom makes a transition from *n* = 3 to *n* = 2 orbit. Calculate the wavelength of the photon emitted. (Given R = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹)

---

## SECTION C

*(This section contains 7 questions of 3 marks each.)*

**22.** (a) Derive an expression for the electric field at a point on the axial line of an electric dipole. (2 marks)

(b) An electric dipole with dipole moment p = 4 × 10⁻⁹ C·m is aligned at 30° with the direction of a uniform electric field of magnitude 5 × 10⁴ N/C. Find the magnitude of the torque acting on the dipole. (1 mark)

---

**23.** (a) Using the concept of drift velocity, derive Ohm's law. (2 marks)

(b) A wire of resistance 4R is bent in the form of a circle. What is the effective resistance between the ends of a diameter? (1 mark)

---

**24.** Explain the principle and working of a moving coil galvanometer with the help of a labelled diagram. What is the function of the radial magnetic field used in it?

---

**25.** (a) State Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction.

(b) A rectangular coil of area 50 cm² and 100 turns is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.1 T. If the coil is rotated about an axis perpendicular to the field through an angle of 60° in 0.1 s, find the average emf induced in the coil.

**OR**

(a) Derive an expression for the self-inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A, length *l*, and total number of turns N.

(b) Two inductors of self-inductance L₁ = 4 mH and L₂ = 8 mH are connected in series. Calculate the equivalent inductance of the combination. If the current flowing through the combination is 2 A, what is the total energy stored?

---

**26.** (a) What are coherent sources? Why are coherent sources necessary to produce a sustained interference pattern?

(b) In Young's double-slit experiment using monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm, the intensity at a point where the path difference is 900 nm is I₁. Find the intensity at a point where the path difference is 300 nm, in terms of I₁.

---

**27.** (a) Draw the energy band diagrams of (i) an intrinsic semiconductor and (ii) a p-type extrinsic semiconductor.

(b) Explain how the conductivity of a semiconductor changes with increase in temperature. How does this behaviour differ from that of a metallic conductor?

---

**28.** (a) State the law of radioactive decay. Derive the relation N = N₀ e⁻λt, where the symbols have their usual meaning.

(b) Define the terms *half-life* and *mean life* of a radioactive substance. Write the relation between them.

---

## SECTION D

*(This section contains 2 case study–based questions of 4 marks each.)*

**29.** **Case Study: Electromagnetic Induction in Everyday Life**

Electromagnetic induction is the phenomenon in which an emf is induced in a coil whenever the magnetic flux linked with it changes. This principle is used in many devices such as electric generators, transformers, and induction cooktops.

In an induction cooktop, a coil of copper wire is placed beneath the cooking surface. When an alternating current flows through the coil, it produces a rapidly changing magnetic field. This changing magnetic field induces eddy currents in the ferromagnetic base of the cooking vessel placed on the cooktop. These eddy currents heat the vessel due to the Joule heating effect (I²R losses). The cooktop surface itself remains relatively cool, as it is made of a non-metallic material (glass ceramic) which does not get heated by the changing magnetic field.

A typical induction cooktop operates at a frequency of 25 kHz with a coil current of 15 A.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Name the underlying physical principle that makes induction cooking possible. (1 mark)

(ii) Why does the cooking vessel need to be made of a ferromagnetic material? What happens if a copper vessel is used instead? (1 mark)

(iii) Why does the glass ceramic surface of the cooktop not get heated? (1 mark)

(iv) If the frequency of the current in the coil is increased, how will the heating of the cooking vessel be affected? Explain.

**OR**

Explain why eddy currents are considered undesirable in the core of a transformer. How is their effect minimised? (1 mark)

---

**30.** **Case Study: The Photoelectric Effect and Solar Cells**

Albert Einstein received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 for his explanation of the photoelectric effect. According to Einstein's equation, when photons of energy *hν* strike a metal surface, electrons are ejected if the photon energy exceeds the work function (φ) of the metal. The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is given by: K_max = hν − φ.

Solar cells (photovoltaic cells) are devices that convert light energy directly into electrical energy using the photovoltaic effect—a phenomenon closely related to the photoelectric effect. A typical silicon solar cell has a work function of approximately 1.1 eV. When sunlight falls on the p-n junction of the cell, electron-hole pairs are generated. The built-in electric field at the junction separates these charges, creating a potential difference and hence an electric current in the external circuit.

A particular solar cell receives light of wavelength 550 nm and produces a current of 100 mA at a voltage of 0.5 V.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Calculate the energy of each photon in eV falling on the solar cell. (1 mark)

(ii) What is the maximum kinetic energy (in eV) of the photoelectrons emitted from the silicon surface? (1 mark)

(iii) Calculate the power output of the solar cell. (1 mark)

(iv) If the intensity of light falling on the cell is doubled (without changing the wavelength), how will the current and voltage of the cell be affected? Explain.

**OR**

What is the threshold frequency for photoemission from a metal surface with a work function of 2.0 eV? (1 mark)

---

## SECTION E

*(This section contains 3 long answer questions of 5 marks each.)*

**31.** (a) Using Gauss's law, derive an expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell at a point: (i) outside the shell, and (ii) inside the shell. (3 marks)

(b) A spherical conducting shell of inner radius r₁ and outer radius r₂ has a charge Q on it. A point charge q is placed at the centre of the shell. Find the surface charge density on the inner and outer surfaces of the shell. (2 marks)

**OR**

(a) Derive an expression for the energy stored in a charged parallel plate capacitor. Hence obtain the expression for the energy density of the electric field between its plates. (3 marks)

(b) A 12 pF capacitor is connected to a 50 V battery. How much electrostatic energy is stored in the capacitor? If another capacitor of 6 pF is connected in series with it and the combination is connected to the same battery, calculate the energy stored in the combination. (2 marks)

---

**32.** (a) Draw a ray diagram to show refraction of a monochromatic ray of light through a glass prism. Derive the expression for the refractive index of the material of the prism in terms of the angle of prism A and the angle of minimum deviation δₘ. (3 marks)

(b) The angle of a glass prism is 60° and the refractive index of the glass is √3. Calculate the angle of minimum deviation and the angle of incidence at minimum deviation. (2 marks)

**OR**

(a) What is meant by a wavefront? Using Huygens' principle, draw a figure to show how a plane wavefront gets diffracted when it passes through a narrow single slit. Sketch the variation of intensity of the diffracted light with angle θ. (3 marks)

(b) Monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm is incident on a slit of width 0.5 mm. Find the angular position of the first minimum and the angular width of the central maximum of the diffraction pattern formed on a screen. (2 marks)

---

**33.** (a) Draw a labelled circuit diagram showing the use of an n-p-n transistor as a common emitter amplifier. Explain its working. Define the terms *current gain* and *voltage gain* of the amplifier. (3 marks)

(b) In a common emitter amplifier circuit, the input resistance is 1 kΩ and the output resistance is 10 kΩ. If the current gain (β) is 50, calculate: (i) the voltage gain, and (ii) the power gain of the amplifier. (2 marks)

**OR**

(a) What is meant by *rectification*? With the help of a labelled circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the working of a full-wave rectifier using two diodes. (3 marks)

(b) Distinguish between n-type and p-type semiconductors on the basis of (i) the type of dopant used, (ii) the majority charge carriers, and (iii) the energy band diagram. (2 marks)

---

***— End of Paper —***

# CENTRAL BOARD OF SECONDARY EDUCATION

## CLASS XII — PHYSICS (Theory)

### PREDICTION PAPER – 3

---

| | |
|---|---|
| **Time Allowed:** 3 Hours | **Maximum Marks:** 70 |
| **Date:** ____/____/2027 | **Code No. 042** |

---

### General Instructions:

1. There are 33 questions in all. All questions are compulsory.
2. This question paper has five sections: **Section A, Section B, Section C, Section D** and **Section E**.
3. All the sections are compulsory.
4. **Section A** contains sixteen questions, twelve MCQ and four Assertion-Reasoning based of 1 mark each.
5. **Section B** contains five questions of two marks each.
6. **Section C** contains seven questions of three marks each.
7. **Section D** contains two case study–based questions of four marks each.
8. **Section E** contains three long answer questions of five marks each.
9. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in two questions in Section B, one question in Section C and all three questions in Section E. You have to attempt only one of the choices in such questions.
10. Use of calculators is not allowed.
11. You may use the following values of physical constants wherever necessary:

$$c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$$

$$e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

$$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}$$

$$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2\text{/N·m}^2$$

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m}^2\text{/C}^2$$

$$m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$$

$$m_n = 1.675 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$

$$m_p = 1.673 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Avogadro's number} = 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text{ per gram mole}$$

$$\text{Boltzmann constant} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$$

---

## SECTION A

*(This section contains 16 questions of 1 mark each.)*

**1.** A hollow metal sphere of radius 10 cm is charged such that the potential on its surface is 80 V. The potential at the centre of the sphere is:

(a) 80 V

(b) 800 V

(c) Zero

(d) 8 V

---

**2.** Three charges +q, +q and −2q are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle. The electric dipole moment of the system is:

(a) Zero

(b) q × side of triangle

(c) q√3 × side of triangle

(d) 2q × side of triangle

---

**3.** Two cells of emf ε₁ and ε₂ and internal resistances r₁ and r₂ respectively are connected in series. The condition for maximum current in an external resistance R is:

(a) R = r₁ + r₂

(b) R = r₁ − r₂

(c) R = 0

(d) R → ∞

---

**4.** A long straight wire carrying a current I is bent at its midpoint to form an angle of 90°. The magnetic field at a point on the angle bisector at a distance *d* from the midpoint is:

(a) μ₀I/(4πd)

(b) μ₀I/(2πd)

(c) μ₀I(√2)/(4πd)

(d) μ₀I/(2√2 πd)

---

**5.** The angle of dip at a place where the horizontal and vertical components of the Earth's magnetic field are equal is:

(a) 0°

(b) 30°

(c) 45°

(d) 90°

---

**6.** A 100 mH inductor carries a current of 1 A. The energy stored in the magnetic field is:

(a) 0.05 J

(b) 0.5 J

(c) 1.0 J

(d) 0.1 J

---

**7.** In an AC circuit, the rms voltage is 220 V. The peak voltage is approximately:

(a) 155.5 V

(b) 220 V

(c) 311 V

(d) 440 V

---

**8.** Which of the following is NOT a property of electromagnetic waves?

(a) They are transverse in nature

(b) They can be polarised

(c) They require a material medium for propagation

(d) They carry energy and momentum

---

**9.** The refractive index of a medium with respect to vacuum depends on:

(a) Only the nature of the medium

(b) Only the wavelength of light

(c) Both the nature of the medium and the wavelength of light

(d) Neither the nature of the medium nor the wavelength of light

---

**10.** In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the central maximum is:

(a) Proportional to the slit width

(b) Inversely proportional to the slit width

(c) Independent of the slit width

(d) Proportional to the square of the slit width

---

**11.** The stopping potential for a metal surface is V₀ when light of frequency ν is incident on it. If the frequency of the incident light is doubled, the stopping potential becomes:

(a) V₀

(b) 2V₀

(c) More than 2V₀

(d) Less than 2V₀

---

**12.** The number of neutrons in the nucleus of ₉₂U²³⁸ is:

(a) 92

(b) 146

(c) 238

(d) 330

---

**For Questions 13 to 16, two statements are given—one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (a), (b), (c) and (d) as given below:**

(a) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

(b) Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.

(c) A is true but R is false.

(d) A is false and R is also false.

---

**13.** **Assertion (A):** The potential difference across the terminals of a battery is always less than its emf when current is drawn from it.

**Reason (R):** Some potential is dropped across the internal resistance of the battery.

---

**14.** **Assertion (A):** A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction.

**Reason (R):** A changing current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic flux, which induces an emf in the secondary coil.

---

**15.** **Assertion (A):** The photoelectric current increases with increase in intensity of incident light (above threshold frequency).

**Reason (R):** Increasing the intensity of light increases the number of photons striking the surface per unit time.

---

**16.** **Assertion (A):** The intrinsic semiconductor becomes an insulator at absolute zero temperature.

**Reason (R):** At absolute zero temperature, no electrons from the valence band can cross the energy gap to reach the conduction band.

---

## SECTION B

*(This section contains 5 questions of 2 marks each.)*

**17.** Two point charges 5 μC and −5 μC are placed 0.1 m apart. Calculate the electric field at a point on the perpendicular bisector of the line joining the charges at a distance 0.1 m from the midpoint.

---

**18.** Define the term *magnetic susceptibility*. What is its significance? Distinguish between diamagnetic and paramagnetic materials on the basis of magnetic susceptibility.

**OR**

A horizontal straight wire of length 0.3 m carrying a current of 5 A is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.1 T directed vertically downward. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the force on the wire.

---

**19.** (a) What is self-induction? Write the SI unit of self-inductance.

(b) A current of 2.5 A flows through a coil of inductance 5 H. Find the magnetic flux linked with the coil. If this current is completely reversed in 0.1 s, find the average emf induced.

**OR**

A series LCR circuit with L = 0.12 H, C = 480 nF, and R = 23 Ω is connected to a 230 V variable frequency supply. Find the resonant frequency and the current at resonance.

---

**20.** (a) Define the term *wavefront*. State the geometrical shape of the wavefront when (i) a point source is at a very large distance, and (ii) a point source is at a finite distance.

(b) State Huygens' principle.

---

**21.** An alpha particle and a proton are accelerated through the same potential difference. Find the ratio of their de Broglie wavelengths. (Given: mass of alpha particle = 4 times mass of proton, charge of alpha particle = 2 times charge of proton)

---

## SECTION C

*(This section contains 7 questions of 3 marks each.)*

**22.** (a) Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor. (2 marks)

(b) Three capacitors of capacitances 2 μF, 3 μF, and 6 μF are connected first in series and then in parallel. Find the ratio of the equivalent capacitances in the two cases. (1 mark)

---

**23.** (a) State and explain Kirchhoff's junction rule and loop rule.

(b) In the given circuit, find the current through the 3 Ω resistor:

*A circuit consisting of a 9 V battery connected to two resistors in a mixed combination — a 6 Ω resistor is connected in parallel with a series combination of 3 Ω and 3 Ω resistors.*

---

**24.** (a) Derive an expression for the force per unit length between two long, straight, parallel conductors carrying currents I₁ and I₂ in the same direction. Is the force attractive or repulsive?

(b) Define one ampere using this expression.

---

**25.** (a) Draw a labelled diagram of an AC generator and explain its working principle. Derive the expression for the instantaneous emf generated.

(b) A coil of 100 turns, each of area 0.02 m², is rotated at a constant angular speed of 300 revolutions per minute about an axis in the plane of the coil and perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T. Calculate the peak value of the emf generated.

**OR**

(a) Describe the construction and working of a transformer. Derive the relation between the voltages and the number of turns of the primary and secondary coils.

(b) A step-down transformer transforms 2200 V to 220 V. The primary coil has 5000 turns. Calculate the number of turns in the secondary coil. If the transformer draws a current of 1.5 A from the mains supply, calculate the current in the secondary coil (assuming 100% efficiency).

---

**26.** (a) State two features of the photoelectric effect that cannot be explained by the wave theory of light.

(b) The work function of cesium is 2.14 eV. Find (i) the threshold frequency for cesium, and (ii) the wavelength of the incident light if the photocurrent is brought to zero by a stopping potential of 0.60 V.

---

**27.** (a) Draw the circuit diagram of a Zener diode as a voltage regulator. Explain its working.

(b) A Zener diode has a breakdown voltage of 9.1 V. It is connected in series with a resistance of 5 kΩ across a supply of 15 V. Calculate the current through the Zener diode if the load resistance connected in parallel with the diode is 10 kΩ.

---

**28.** (a) Draw the energy level diagram of a hydrogen atom showing the first four energy levels. Show the transitions corresponding to the Lyman and Balmer series.

(b) Calculate the shortest wavelength of the Balmer series. In which region of the electromagnetic spectrum does this wavelength fall? (Given R = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹)

---

## SECTION D

*(This section contains 2 case study–based questions of 4 marks each.)*

**29.** **Case Study: MRI — Magnetic Resonance Imaging**

Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a non-invasive medical imaging technique that uses a powerful magnetic field, radio waves, and a computer to produce detailed images of internal body structures. The patient is placed inside a large cylindrical magnet that creates a strong, uniform magnetic field — typically between 1.5 T and 3.0 T. This field is produced by a superconducting solenoid cooled to about 4 K using liquid helium.

The hydrogen nuclei (protons) in the body, which possess a magnetic moment, tend to align either parallel or anti-parallel to the external field. A radiofrequency (RF) pulse is then applied at the resonant frequency (called the Larmor frequency), which causes some protons to flip to a higher energy state. When the RF pulse is switched off, the protons relax back to their original alignment, emitting RF signals that are detected and processed to form an image.

The Larmor frequency is given by f = γB/(2π), where γ = 2.675 × 10⁸ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ is the gyromagnetic ratio for hydrogen protons and B is the magnetic field strength.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Calculate the Larmor frequency for hydrogen protons in a 1.5 T magnetic field. (1 mark)

(ii) Why is a superconducting solenoid used to produce the magnetic field instead of an ordinary electromagnet? (1 mark)

(iii) When a proton aligns anti-parallel to the magnetic field, is it in a higher or lower energy state compared to parallel alignment? Justify. (1 mark)

(iv) If the magnetic field strength is doubled from 1.5 T to 3.0 T, how does the Larmor frequency change?

**OR**

Name the physical principle on which the detection of MRI signals is based. How does this relate to electromagnetic induction? (1 mark)

---

**30.** **Case Study: Nuclear Power and Mass-Energy Equivalence**

Nuclear power plants generate electricity through nuclear fission reactions. In a typical fission reaction, a heavy nucleus such as Uranium-235 absorbs a slow (thermal) neutron and splits into two intermediate-mass fragments along with a few neutrons and a large amount of energy. A representative fission reaction is:

$$_{92}^{235}\text{U} + _{0}^{1}\text{n} \rightarrow _{56}^{141}\text{Ba} + _{36}^{92}\text{Kr} + 3\,_{0}^{1}\text{n} + Q$$

The energy released Q comes from the mass defect — the difference between the total mass of the reactants and the total mass of the products. This mass difference is converted into energy according to Einstein's relation E = mc².

The following masses are given:
- m(²³⁵U) = 235.0439 u
- m(¹⁴¹Ba) = 140.9144 u
- m(⁹²Kr) = 91.9262 u
- m(n) = 1.0087 u
- 1 u = 931.5 MeV/c²

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Calculate the mass defect (Δm) for the given fission reaction in atomic mass units (u). (1 mark)

(ii) Calculate the energy released Q in the fission of one uranium-235 nucleus (in MeV). (1 mark)

(iii) Why are slow (thermal) neutrons more effective than fast neutrons in causing fission of U-235? (1 mark)

(iv) If 1 kg of U-235 undergoes complete fission, estimate the total energy released. (Avogadro's number = 6.023 × 10²³)

**OR**

What is the role of the moderator and control rods in a nuclear reactor? (1 mark)

---

## SECTION E

*(This section contains 3 long answer questions of 5 marks each.)*

**31.** (a) Derive an expression for the electric potential at a general point due to a short electric dipole. Hence, obtain the expression for the potential on (i) the axial line and (ii) the equatorial line of the dipole. (3 marks)

(b) An electric dipole of dipole moment 2 × 10⁻⁶ C·m is placed in a uniform electric field of 10⁵ N/C.

(i) What is the maximum torque experienced by the dipole?

(ii) What is the potential energy of the dipole when it makes an angle of 90° with the field direction? (2 marks)

**OR**

(a) Derive an expression for the equivalent capacitance when three capacitors C₁, C₂, and C₃ are connected (i) in series, and (ii) in parallel. (3 marks)

(b) In the given network, four capacitors each of 12 μF capacitance are connected to a 500 V supply. Determine the equivalent capacitance and the charge on each capacitor if the network consists of two capacitors in series, and this combination is connected in parallel with two more capacitors in series.

*[Two branches in parallel between points A and B: each branch has two 12 μF capacitors in series.]* (2 marks)

---

**32.** (a) Draw a labelled ray diagram showing image formation by a compound microscope when the final image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision (D = 25 cm). Derive the expression for its total magnifying power. (3 marks)

(b) A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length 1.25 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 5 cm. When an object is placed at a distance of 1.51 cm from the objective, the final image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision. Calculate the magnifying power of the microscope. (2 marks)

**OR**

(a) What is meant by *polarisation* of light? State and explain Brewster's law. Hence prove that when light is incident at the Brewster angle, the reflected and refracted rays are mutually perpendicular. (3 marks)

(b) Unpolarised light of intensity I₀ passes through two Polaroids. The transmission axis of the second Polaroid makes an angle of 60° with that of the first. Find the intensity of the light emerging from the second Polaroid. If a third Polaroid (with its transmission axis at 30° to the first) is placed between the two, find the intensity of light emerging from the system. (2 marks)

---

**33.** (a) With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a common emitter n-p-n transistor amplifier. Draw the input and output waveforms. Define *current amplification factor (β)* in common emitter configuration. (3 marks)

(b) For a CE transistor amplifier, the audio signal voltage across the collector resistance of 2 kΩ is 2 V. If the current amplification factor of the transistor is 100 and the base resistance is 1 kΩ, find the input signal voltage, base current, and the collector current. (2 marks)

**OR**

(a) Explain with the help of a suitable diagram the working of a p-n junction diode as a half-wave rectifier. Draw the input and output waveforms. Why is a full-wave rectifier preferred over a half-wave rectifier? (3 marks)

(b) Explain the working of a light-emitting diode (LED). What is the main advantage of using LEDs over conventional incandescent lamps? Give any two common applications of LEDs. (2 marks)

---

***— End of Paper —***
